content.content:
=== PLAN ===
# pl24r — Pascal P-Code Linker in Rust

## Goal

Build a Rust CLI tool that combines multiple `.spc` (symbolic p-code assembler) text files into one merged `.spc` file for pasm assembly. This is a Phase 1 offline text-level linker for the Pascal-on-COR24 toolchain.

## Pipeline

```
Pascal source(s) → p24p compiler → .spc file(s)  ─┐
Hand-written runtime .spc (phase0)                 ├→ pl24r linker → combined .spc → pasm → .p24 → emulator
Pascal-compiled runtime .spc (phase1+)             ┘
```

## Input categories

1. **App** — the main program module (compiled from Pascal by p24p)
2. **User library** (optional) — separately compiled Pascal library
3. **Phase 0 runtime** — hand-written .spc (write_int, write_ln, write_bool)
4. **Phase 1+ runtime** — Pascal-compiled runtime routines

## Module metadata format (co-designed with pv24a/p24p)

New `.spc` directives for linking:

- `.module Name` — declare module identity
- `.export sym` — mark symbol as visible to other modules
- `.extern sym` — declare external symbol dependency
- `.endmodule` — end module boundary

**Fallback**: files without metadata are treated as export-all (all `.proc`, `.global`, `.data` symbols exported).

## Output

One combined `.spc` text file with all modules merged, all symbols resolved, ready for pasm. No `.pco` or `.pex` intermediate formats.

## Design constraints

- Text in, text out — no binary formats
- Transport-neutral (files, stdin, pipes)
- Line-oriented, forward-readable
- Entry point: the `main` proc from the app module
- Duplicate symbol detection with clear errors
- Unresolved extern detection with clear errors

## Steps

1. **001-spc-parser** — Rust project scaffold + .spc text parser
2. **002-symbol-table** — Symbol collection, export/import resolution, validation
3. **003-linker-core** — Module merging, ordering, combined .spc emission
4. **004-cli-integration** — CLI interface, file/stream modes, error reporting
5. **005-pipeline-test** — End-to-end test with real runtime + app through full pipeline

=== STEPS ===
  [ ] 001-spc-parser [production]: Create the pl24r Rust linker project scaffold with edition 2024. Set up src/m... <-- YOU ARE HERE
  [ ] 002-symbol-table [production]: Build the symbol table and validation layer in src/symbols.rs. Collect all sy...
  [ ] 003-linker-core [production]: Implement the core linker in src/linker.rs that merges multiple parsed .spc m...
  [ ] 004-cli-integration [production]: Build the CLI interface in src/main.rs. Support two modes: (1) file mode: pl2...
  [ ] 005-pipeline-test [production]: End-to-end integration test with the real Pascal toolchain. (1) Copy or refer...

=== CURRENT STEP: 001-spc-parser [production] ===
Status: pending
Description: Create the pl24r Rust linker project scaffold with edition 2024. Set up src/m...
Task type: rust-project-init

=== PROMPT ===
Create the pl24r Rust linker project scaffold with edition 2024. Set up src/main.rs as CLI entry point, src/lib.rs with module structure, and src/parser.rs with a complete .spc text parser. The parser must handle all current .spc directives (.proc NAME nlocals, .end, .global NAME nwords, .data NAME byte,byte,..., .const NAME value), labels (name:), instructions (mnemonic operand), and comments (; ...). Also parse the new module metadata directives: .module NAME, .endmodule, .export SYM, .extern SYM. Parse into a structured AST: Module { name, exports, externs, items: Vec<Item> } where Item is Proc/Global/Data/Const/Comment. For files without .module/.endmodule, infer module name from filename and treat all symbols as exported (export-all fallback). Test: cargo test with unit tests for parsing each directive type, a complete hello.spc-style file, and a file with module metadata. cargo clippy -- -D warnings must pass clean. Reference ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/pv24a/hello.spc and ~/github/softwarewrighter/pr24p/src/runtime.spc for real .spc examples.

=== WHEN DONE ===
Run: agentrail begin    (if not yet started)
Run: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --next-slug <slug> --next-prompt "next instructions"
  Or: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --done   (if this is the last step)

=== PLAN ===
# pl24r — Pascal P-Code Linker in Rust

## Goal

Build a Rust CLI tool that combines multiple `.spc` (symbolic p-code assembler) text files into one merged `.spc` file for pasm assembly. This is a Phase 1 offline text-level linker for the Pascal-on-COR24 toolchain.

## Pipeline

```
Pascal source(s) → p24p compiler → .spc file(s)  ─┐
Hand-written runtime .spc (phase0)                 ├→ pl24r linker → combined .spc → pasm → .p24 → emulator
Pascal-compiled runtime .spc (phase1+)             ┘
```

## Input categories

1. **App** — the main program module (compiled from Pascal by p24p)
2. **User library** (optional) — separately compiled Pascal library
3. **Phase 0 runtime** — hand-written .spc (write_int, write_ln, write_bool)
4. **Phase 1+ runtime** — Pascal-compiled runtime routines

## Module metadata format (co-designed with pv24a/p24p)

New `.spc` directives for linking:

- `.module Name` — declare module identity
- `.export sym` — mark symbol as visible to other modules
- `.extern sym` — declare external symbol dependency
- `.endmodule` — end module boundary

**Fallback**: files without metadata are treated as export-all (all `.proc`, `.global`, `.data` symbols exported).

## Output

One combined `.spc` text file with all modules merged, all symbols resolved, ready for pasm. No `.pco` or `.pex` intermediate formats.

## Design constraints

- Text in, text out — no binary formats
- Transport-neutral (files, stdin, pipes)
- Line-oriented, forward-readable
- Entry point: the `main` proc from the app module
- Duplicate symbol detection with clear errors
- Unresolved extern detection with clear errors

## Steps

1. **001-spc-parser** — Rust project scaffold + .spc text parser
2. **002-symbol-table** — Symbol collection, export/import resolution, validation
3. **003-linker-core** — Module merging, ordering, combined .spc emission
4. **004-cli-integration** — CLI interface, file/stream modes, error reporting
5. **005-pipeline-test** — End-to-end test with real runtime + app through full pipeline

file.content:
; hello.spc — Hello World for pv24a P-Code VM
;
; Expected output: Hello\n

.data msg 72, 101, 108, 108, 111, 10, 0

.proc main 0
    push msg
    call puts
    halt
.end

.proc puts 1
    loada 0
    storel 0
loop:
    loadl 0
    loadb
    dup
    jz done
    sys 1
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add
    storel 0
    jmp loop
done:
    drop
    ret 1
.end

content.content:
1→; pr24p — Pascal Runtime Library
     2→; Phase 0: Hand-written .spc stubs for p-code VM syscall wrappers
     3→
     4→; _p24p_write_int ( n -- )
     5→; Print signed integer to UART as decimal.
     6→; Handles negative numbers (prints '-' prefix).
     7→; Uses div/mod by 10 to extract digits, pushes onto eval stack
     8→; in reverse order with a zero sentinel, then prints from most
     9→; significant to least significant via sys 1 (PUTC).
    10→.proc _p24p_write_int 1
    11→    enter 1
    12→    loada 0              ; load argument n
    13→    ; check for negative
    14→    dup                  ; n n
    15→    push 0               ; n n 0
    16→    lt                   ; n (n<0?)
    17→    jz positive
    18→    ; print minus sign
    19→    push 45              ; n '-'
    20→    sys 1                ; n
    21→    neg                  ; -n
    22→positive:
    23→    storel 0             ; local0 = abs(n)
    24→    push 0               ; push sentinel
    25→extract:
    26→    loadl 0              ; load n
    27→    push 10
    28→    mod                  ; n % 10
    29→    push 48
    30→    add                  ; n % 10 + '0' = digit char
    31→    loadl 0
    32→    push 10
    33→    div                  ; n / 10
    34→    storel 0             ; update n = n / 10
    35→    loadl 0
    36→    jnz extract          ; if n != 0, extract more digits
    37→print:
    38→    dup
    39→    jz done
    40→    sys 1                ; PUTC digit
    41→    jmp print
    42→done:
    43→    drop                 ; discard sentinel
    44→    leave
    45→    ret 1
    46→.end
    47→

file.content:
; pr24p — Pascal Runtime Library
; Phase 0: Hand-written .spc stubs for p-code VM syscall wrappers

; _p24p_write_int ( n -- )
; Print signed integer to UART as decimal.
; Handles negative numbers (prints '-' prefix).
; Uses div/mod by 10 to extract digits, pushes onto eval stack
; in reverse order with a zero sentinel, then prints from most
; significant to least significant via sys 1 (PUTC).
.proc _p24p_write_int 1
    enter 1
    loada 0              ; load argument n
    ; check for negative
    dup                  ; n n
    push 0               ; n n 0
    lt                   ; n (n<0?)
    jz positive
    ; print minus sign
    push 45              ; n '-'
    sys 1                ; n
    neg                  ; -n
positive:
    storel 0             ; local0 = abs(n)
    push 0               ; push sentinel
extract:
    loadl 0              ; load n
    push 10
    mod                  ; n % 10
    push 48
    add                  ; n % 10 + '0' = digit char
    loadl 0
    push 10
    div                  ; n / 10
    storel 0             ; update n = n / 10
    loadl 0
    jnz extract          ; if n != 0, extract more digits
print:
    dup
    jz done
    sys 1                ; PUTC digit
    jmp print
done:
    drop                 ; discard sentinel
    leave
    ret 1
.end

     5→So I have a p-code VM running on an emulator and I'm building a Pascal compiler that compiles Pascal runtime routines (written in Pascal) to a p-code file and compiles an App (demo or test) to a p-code file.  What is the mechanism for loading, "linking," and running these?  Can a user also compile a Pascal library (user library) and combine that?  What are the conventions? Is there actual module supporting syntax?  Do compiled p-code contain linking info?  How would a p-code emulator handle this?  Is it as simple as concatenating all the p-code files into one file, or does the emulator need to manage and load (and link?) each p-code file at start-up?  i.e. take a list of p-code files at startup? 
     6→
     7→You have:
     8→
     9→a Pascal compiler that emits p-code
    10→
    11→a p-code VM/emulator
    12→
    13→runtime routines written in Pascal and compiled to p-code
    14→
    15→an application/demo/test also compiled to p-code
    16→
    17→possibly user libraries compiled separately
    18→
    19→So the real design question is not just “how do I load files,” but:
    20→
    21→what unit of compilation is
    22→
    23→what unit of linking is
    24→
    25→where symbol resolution happens
    26→
    27→what the VM is expected to know
    28→
    29→whether separate compilation is worth the complexity yet
    30→
    31→High-level answer
    32→For an educational Pascal-on-p-code system, the cleanest progression is:
    33→
    34→Phase 1
    35→Use a linker outside the VM.
    36→
    37→Compile each source file/module/library to an object-like p-code file
    38→
    39→Run a p-code linker
    40→
    41→Produce one final executable p-code image
    42→
    43→The VM loads and runs that one fully linked image
    44→
    45→That is the simplest, most conventional design for your stage.
    46→
    47→Phase 2
    48→Add separate compilation units with symbol tables/import-export metadata.
    49→
    50→runtime.pco
    51→
    52→mylib.pco
    53→
    54→app.pco
    55→
    56→linker resolves references
    57→
    58→outputs app.px or app.exe.pcode
    59→
    60→Phase 3
    61→Only later, if you want, support dynamic loading or VM-side loading of multiple p-code files.
    62→
    63→That is usually not the first thing to build.
    64→
    65→Recommended architecture
    66→1. Treat compiled p-code files in two categories
    67→A. Relocatable p-code object
    68→This is like .o in C toolchains.
    69→
    70→It contains:

file.content:
So I have a p-code VM running on an emulator and I'm building a Pascal compiler that compiles Pascal runtime routines (written in Pascal) to a p-code file and compiles an App (demo or test) to a p-code file.  What is the mechanism for loading, "linking," and running these?  Can a user also compile a Pascal library (user library) and combine that?  What are the conventions? Is there actual module supporting syntax?  Do compiled p-code contain linking info?  How would a p-code emulator handle this?  Is it as simple as concatenating all the p-code files into one file, or does the emulator need to manage and load (and link?) each p-code file at start-up?  i.e. take a list of p-code files at startup? 

You have:

a Pascal compiler that emits p-code

a p-code VM/emulator

runtime routines written in Pascal and compiled to p-code

an application/demo/test also compiled to p-code

possibly user libraries compiled separately

So the real design question is not just “how do I load files,” but:

what unit of compilation is

what unit of linking is

where symbol resolution happens

what the VM is expected to know

whether separate compilation is worth the complexity yet

High-level answer
For an educational Pascal-on-p-code system, the cleanest progression is:

Phase 1
Use a linker outside the VM.

Compile each source file/module/library to an object-like p-code file

Run a p-code linker

Produce one final executable p-code image

The VM loads and runs that one fully linked image

That is the simplest, most conventional design for your stage.

Phase 2
Add separate compilation units with symbol tables/import-export metadata.

runtime.pco

mylib.pco

app.pco

linker resolves references

outputs app.px or app.exe.pcode

Phase 3
Only later, if you want, support dynamic loading or VM-side loading of multiple p-code files.

That is usually not the first thing to build.

Recommended architecture
1. Treat compiled p-code files in two categories
A. Relocatable p-code object
This is like .o in C toolchains.

It contains:

p-code instructions

constant data

static/global variable layout

symbol definitions

unresolved external references

5:The p-code VM maintains the following state in a memory struct:
9:    .word 0     ; pc   — p-code instruction pointer
10:    .word 0     ; esp  — eval stack pointer (top of eval stack)
11:    .word 0     ; csp  — call stack pointer (top of call stack)
12:    .word 0     ; fp   — current frame pointer (call stack)
13:    .word 0     ; gp   — globals base address
14:    .word 0     ; hp   — heap pointer (next free)
15:    .word 0     ; code — base address of p-code segment
16:    .word 0     ; status — 0=running, 1=halted, 2=trapped
17:    .word 0     ; trap_code — last trap reason
26:| `push` imm24 | 4 bytes | ( -- n ) | Push 24-bit immediate |
27:| `push_s` imm8 | 2 bytes | ( -- n ) | Push sign-extended 8-bit immediate |
28:| `dup` | 1 byte | ( a -- a a ) | Duplicate top |
29:| `drop` | 1 byte | ( a -- ) | Discard top |
30:| `swap` | 1 byte | ( a b -- b a ) | Swap top two |
37:| `add` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a+b ) | Add |
38:| `sub` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a-b ) | Subtract |
39:| `mul` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a*b ) | Multiply |
40:| `div` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a/b ) | Divide (trap on b=0) |
41:| `mod` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a%b ) | Modulo (trap on b=0) |
42:| `neg` | 1 byte | ( a -- -a ) | Negate |
43:| `and` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a&b ) | Bitwise AND |
44:| `or` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a\|b ) | Bitwise OR |
45:| `xor` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a^b ) | Bitwise XOR |
46:| `not` | 1 byte | ( a -- ~a ) | Bitwise NOT |
47:| `shl` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a<<n ) | Shift left |
52:All comparisons push 1 (true) or 0 (false):
56:| `eq` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Equal |
57:| `ne` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Not equal |
58:| `lt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less than (signed) |
59:| `le` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less or equal (signed) |
60:| `gt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater than (signed) |
61:| `ge` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater or equal (signed) |
67:| `jmp` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( -- ) | Unconditional jump |
68:| `jz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if zero |
69:| `jnz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if nonzero |
70:| `call` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( args... -- ) | Call procedure (static link = 0) |
71:| `calln` depth8 addr24 | 5 bytes | ( args... -- ) | Call with static link (depth=0: nested, depth=N: N levels up) |
72:| `ret` nargs8 | 2 bytes | ( [retval] -- [retval] ) | Return, clean nargs |
73:| `halt` | 1 byte | ( -- ) | Stop VM |
74:| `trap` code8 | 2 bytes | ( -- ) | Trigger trap |
80:| `enter` nlocals8 | 2 bytes | ( -- ) | Set up frame, reserve N local slots |
81:| `leave` | 1 byte | ( -- ) | Tear down frame |
82:| `loadl` off8 | 2 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load local at offset |
83:| `storel` off8 | 2 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store local at offset |
84:| `loadg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load global at offset |
85:| `storeg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store global at offset |
86:| `addrl` off8 | 2 bytes | ( -- addr ) | Push address of local |
87:| `addrg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( -- addr ) | Push address of global |
89:| `storen` depth8 off8 | 3 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store nonlocal via static link chain |
91:### 2.6 Indirect Memory Access
95:| `load` | 1 byte | ( addr -- val ) | Load word from address |
96:| `store` | 1 byte | ( val addr -- ) | Store word to address |
97:| `loadb` | 1 byte | ( addr -- byte ) | Load byte (zero-extended) |
98:| `storeb` | 1 byte | ( byte addr -- ) | Store byte |
104:| `sys` id8 | 2 bytes | (varies) | System call |
106:System call IDs:
115:| 5 | FREE | ( ptr -- ) | Free heap block (no-op in bump mode) |
121:| `loada` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load argument by index |
122:| `storea` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store argument by index |

37:| `add` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a+b ) | Add |
38:| `sub` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a-b ) | Subtract |
39:| `mul` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a*b ) | Multiply |
40:| `div` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a/b ) | Divide (trap on b=0) |
41:| `mod` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a%b ) | Modulo (trap on b=0) |
42:| `neg` | 1 byte | ( a -- -a ) | Negate |
43:| `and` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a&b ) | Bitwise AND |
44:| `or` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a\|b ) | Bitwise OR |
45:| `xor` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a^b ) | Bitwise XOR |
46:| `not` | 1 byte | ( a -- ~a ) | Bitwise NOT |
47:| `shl` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a<<n ) | Shift left |
52:All comparisons push 1 (true) or 0 (false):
56:| `eq` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Equal |
57:| `ne` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Not equal |
58:| `lt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less than (signed) |
59:| `le` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less or equal (signed) |
60:| `gt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater than (signed) |
61:| `ge` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater or equal (signed) |
67:| `jmp` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( -- ) | Unconditional jump |
68:| `jz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if zero |
69:| `jnz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if nonzero |
70:| `call` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( args... -- ) | Call procedure (static link = 0) |
71:| `calln` depth8 addr24 | 5 bytes | ( args... -- ) | Call with static link (depth=0: nested, depth=N: N levels up) |
72:| `ret` nargs8 | 2 bytes | ( [retval] -- [retval] ) | Return, clean nargs |
73:| `halt` | 1 byte | ( -- ) | Stop VM |
74:| `trap` code8 | 2 bytes | ( -- ) | Trigger trap |
80:| `enter` nlocals8 | 2 bytes | ( -- ) | Set up frame, reserve N local slots |
81:| `leave` | 1 byte | ( -- ) | Tear down frame |
82:| `loadl` off8 | 2 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load local at offset |
83:| `storel` off8 | 2 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store local at offset |
84:| `loadg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load global at offset |
85:| `storeg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store global at offset |
86:| `addrl` off8 | 2 bytes | ( -- addr ) | Push address of local |
87:| `addrg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( -- addr ) | Push address of global |
89:| `storen` depth8 off8 | 3 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store nonlocal via static link chain |
91:### 2.6 Indirect Memory Access
95:| `load` | 1 byte | ( addr -- val ) | Load word from address |
96:| `store` | 1 byte | ( val addr -- ) | Store word to address |
97:| `loadb` | 1 byte | ( addr -- byte ) | Load byte (zero-extended) |
98:| `storeb` | 1 byte | ( byte addr -- ) | Store byte |
104:| `sys` id8 | 2 bytes | (varies) | System call |
106:System call IDs:
115:| 5 | FREE | ( ptr -- ) | Free heap block (no-op in bump mode) |
121:| `loada` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load argument by index |
122:| `storea` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store argument by index |

content.text: Good, I have a clear picture of the .spc format. Let me build the project.

content.text: All 17 tests pass, clippy clean. Now commit and complete the step.

input.command:
git add src/lib.rs src/parser.rs src/main.rs && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(parser): implement .spc text parser with module metadata support

Scaffold Rust project with src/lib.rs, src/parser.rs, and src/main.rs.
Parser handles all .spc directives (.proc, .end, .global, .data, .const),
labels, instructions with operands and inline comments, and the new
module metadata directives (.module, .endmodule, .export, .extern).
Files without metadata use export-all fallback with name inferred from
filename. 17 unit tests covering each directive type, hello.spc-style
files, runtime.spc parsing, and error cases.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"
